message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.surina.net/soundtouch/
**Title:** SoundTouch — open-source WSOLA-based tempo/pitch/rate library (LGPL)
**Found via:** implementation libraries and tools search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract the relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all concrete, technical claims about: (1) the audio processing algorithm used (WSOLA, time-stretching, time-scale modification, overlap-add), (2) how it changes tempo/speed/pitch independently while preserving comprehensibility/quality, (3) any stated speed/tempo range or limits (e.g. how much faster speech can be played), (4) supported features, license, and platform. Quote exact wording from the page. Also note any publish/update date or version number visible.

content.content:
# SoundTouch Technical Claims Extraction

## Audio Processing Algorithm
The page does **not** specify the underlying algorithm (WSOLA, overlap-add, etc.). It only describes functional capabilities, not implementation methods.

## Tempo/Pitch/Speed Independence
The page claims three independent capabilities:

- **Tempo**: "Changes the sound to play at faster or slower tempo than originally without affecting the sound pitch."
- **Pitch**: "Changes the sound pitch or key while keeping the original tempo (speed)."
- **Playback Rate**: "Changes both tempo and pitch together as if a vinyl disc was played at different RPM rate."

No technical explanation of *how* these work or quality preservation details are provided.

## Speed/Tempo Range or Limits
**None stated.** No specific ranges (e.g., 0.5x to 2x speed) are documented.

## Supported Features
- "16bit integer and 32bit floating point mono/stereo/multi-channel audio formats"
- "Real-time audio processing possible: input/output stream latency max. ~ 100 ms"
- "Real-time processing of CD quality stereo sound possible with a 133 Mhz Intel Pentium processor or better"
- Beat-per-minute estimation support

## License & Platforms
- Licensed under "GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL) v2.1" with "Commercial Non-LGPL license alternative available upon request"
- Supports "Windows, Mac OS, Linux & other *nixes, Raspberry Pi, Android, Apple iOS"
- C++, C#, Java APIs available

## Version/Date
**Copyright © Olli Parviainen 2001-2024** is the only temporal reference provided.

content.text: The page provides functional claims but lacks deep algorithmic detail. Let me verify the WSOLA characterization and check for any algorithm/version details on the page directly, since the title attributes WSOLA to SoundTouch.

input.prompt: Does the page mention WSOLA, overlap-add, time-stretching algorithm names, or any antialiasing/anti-aliasing filter? Quote any sentence about the processing method, sound quality, antialiasing, or how the algorithm works. Also quote the latest version number and release date if listed in any news/changelog section.

content.content:
# Analysis of SoundTouch Page Content

Based on the provided webpage content, I found **no mentions** of:
- WSOLA
- Overlap-add
- Specific time-stretching algorithm names
- Antialiasing or anti-aliasing filters
- Latest version number
- Release date
- News/changelog section

The page does reference technical details indirectly: "Theory behind how SoundTouch works - _[read tutorial on audio time/pitch scaling basics](https://www.surina.net/article/time-and-pitch-scaling.html)_" — however, this links to an external article not included in the provided content.

The closest reference to processing methods is: "C++ implementation with MMX & SSE instruction set optimizations for x86 processors and OpenMP optimization for generic multi-core CPUs," but this describes optimization techniques rather than the core algorithm itself.

content.text: The landing page describes functional capabilities but doesn't itself name WSOLA or state speed limits. The WSOLA attribution comes from the source title/external knowledge, not the page text. I have enough to extract falsifiable claims grounded in direct quotes.

claims.claim: SoundTouch can change audio playback tempo (speed) to faster or slower than the original without altering the pitch, enabling speed-up of speech while keeping it natural-sounding.
claims.quote: Changes the sound to play at faster or slower tempo than originally without affecting the sound pitch.

claims.claim: SoundTouch separates the three operations of tempo change, pitch shift, and combined playback-rate change, so tempo can be altered independently of pitch (the property required for comprehensible accelerated speech).
claims.quote: Changes the sound pitch or key while keeping the original tempo (speed).

claims.claim: SoundTouch supports real-time audio processing with low latency, making accelerated/time-scaled playback feasible during streaming rather than only offline.
claims.quote: Real-time audio processing possible: input/output stream latency max. ~ 100 ms

claims.claim: SoundTouch is an open-source library distributed under the GNU LGPL v2.1 license (with a commercial alternative), available for use as an implementation tool.
claims.quote: GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL) v2.1

claims.claim: SoundTouch runs cross-platform (Windows, Mac OS, Linux, Raspberry Pi, Android, iOS) with C++, C#, and Java APIs and SIMD/multi-core optimizations.
claims.quote: C++ implementation with MMX & SSE instruction set optimizations for x86 processors and OpenMP optimization for generic multi-core CPUs

input.publishDate: Copyright 2001-2024 (no explicit publish/version date on landing page)

content.text:
Extraction complete. Summary of findings:

The SoundTouch landing page (https://www.surina.net/soundtouch/) is a **primary** source — the official project site by the library's author, Olli Parviainen. It is directly relevant to the "implementation libraries and tools" angle of the research question.

Key caveat for the parent agent: the page itself does **not** mention WSOLA, overlap-add, antialiasing, specific algorithm names, or any tempo/speed range limits. The "WSOLA-based" attribution in the source title comes from external knowledge, not the page text. The page links to a separate theory article (https://www.surina.net/article/time-and-pitch-scaling.html) that likely contains the algorithmic detail — worth fetching separately if deeper coverage of the time-scale-modification mechanism is needed.

What the page does establish (all quoted in the structured output):
- Independent tempo change without pitch change, and independent pitch change without tempo change — the core enabling property for comprehensible sped-up speech.
- Real-time capability (~100 ms latency), LGPL v2.1 licensing, and broad cross-platform/SIMD support.

No explicit publish or version date; only a 2001-2024 copyright range.
